data.title: TCW Concentrated Large Cap Growth Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Performance

The Fund (I Share) generated a net return of +5.78% during the fourth quarter, underperforming the Russell 1000 Growth Index return of +7.07%. Relative underperformance was primarily due to negative sector allocation effects (primarily in the consumer discretionary, healthcare, and real estate sectors). For the full year, the Fund (I Share) generated a return of +30.92%, underperforming the Russell 1000 Growth Index return of +33.36%. Negative sector allocation effects outweighed positive security selection effects in CY2024.

Investment Environment

Equity markets moved higher in the fourth quarter. The NASDAQ advanced 6.3%, the S&P 500 +2.4%, and the DJIA +0.5%. The price of crude increased 6.9% and was up 10.1% in 2024. For the quarter, gold dropped 1.6% but was up 20.3% for the year. Economic growth has largely supported the stock market advance, as the U.S. consumer has remained somewhat resilient in the face of sticky inflation and diminished purchasing power. Essential items cost increases (food, clothing, energy, shelter, insurance, utilities) have exceeded headline CPI (Consumer Price Index) in 42 of the past 47 months. Wage increases have failed to keep up with inflation.

Corporate profits remain strong, however, and U.S. investment grade corporate spreads are near multi-decade tights. December’s ISM survey was in contraction territory (49.3), and the most recent U.S. housing starts data (November) indicated housing starts are at the lowest level since July 2024. After a 50 bps rate cut in September, the FOMC cut another 25 bps in each of November and December, and the Federal Reserve (Fed) noted it may only cut rates two more times in 2025. Inflation forecasts moved up for 2025 with the Fed’s preferred inflation measurement, headline PCE (Personal Consumption Expenditures), estimated to increase 2.5% vs 2.1% prior. Fed Chairman Powell’s hawkish commentary in December that the Fed’s year-end inflation forecast has “kind of fallen apart” was noteworthy. As we enter 2025, we are closely monitoring yields on the 10-year UST (up over 100 bps since the Fed’s September rate cut), as well as whether Trump 2.0 leads to deregulation, lower taxes, and an acceleration in economic growth or whether tariffs create unintended consequences.

Sector Weightings

Including the contribution of sector allocation and security selection, our consumer discretionary and financials weightings hurt relative results most, and our information technology and consumer staples weightings most positively impacted relative performance during the fourth quarter.

Security Selection*

Our strongest performance during the period came from the information technology sector. After underperforming the broader market through the first half of 2024, shares of ServiceNow, Inc. (NOW; 7.41%**) moved higher in the fourth quarter after the company reported strong quarterly results in October that topped consensus estimates and the company raised forward guidance. Most of the large deals signed during the quarter were multi-platform (e.g. 18 of top 20 deals included seven products), the Federal business was solid (no impact from Carahsoft, according to management), and the company’s AI SKU (Now Assist) remains the fastest growing product in NOW’s history. With all the focus on hyperscalers in the early innings of this AI boom, there has been consternation amongst some investors around enterprise software and how soon AI investments will be monetized, but NOW is delivering (NOW was able to sign 44 deals over $1 mm, six deals over $5 mm and two over $10 mm). Customers who use Now Assist are receiving tangible returns on their investments. Given the high customer ROI, we believe this positions NOW to capture burgeoning AI spending. The company’s Xanadu release (released in early September) included 350 new GenAI use cases, allowing AI agents to work seamlessly alongside human employees and increase employee productivity. We remain bullish on NOW shares, given our belief the current share price underestimates the long-term earnings power of the company. Shares of Shopify Inc. (SHOP; 2.52%**) rallied after the company reported strong quarterly results that topped consensus estimates. Against a macro backdrop of slowing e-commerce growth, SHOP’s revenue increased 26% YoY, volume growth accelerated, and international growth was robust. Shop Pay volume increased 42% YoY and is approaching half of SHOP’s GPV (Gross Payment Volume). The company continues to add enterprise merchants to the platform, and MRR (Monthly Recurring Revenue) increased 24% YoY. When we first purchased shares earlier this year, we were attracted to the SHOP’s accelerating market share capture, capital-light business model, pricing power and leverage to the secular trend of Direct-to-Consumer retail. We remain positive on shares.

Our weakest relative performance during the fourth quarter came from the information technology sector. Shares of Broadcom Inc. (AVGO; 2.11%**) rallied during the period after the company reported quarterly earnings. (Though we recently initiated our position in the security, our underweight relative to the Russell 1000® Growth Index hurt our relative performance during the quarter.) While results for AVGO’s fiscal fourth quarter largely met consensus estimates, management’s guidance of a potential $60b-$90b in AI revenue in fiscal 2027 from existing customers led to the stock rally. For reference, AVGO’s AI revenue in fiscal 2024 was $12b, so this represents a meaningful increase in AI revenue over the next three years. As large cloud customers continue to build out larger and larger clusters, AVGO stands to benefit from the increased complexity through both custom ASICs and a variety of other leading networking solutions. While we recognize revenue could be lumpy over the next several years, we are attracted to the company’s steady free cash flow generation and disciplined capital allocation, enabled by its market position in a secular growth end market. Shares of ASML Holding NV (ASML; 2.05%**) dropped after the company reported disappointing quarterly results that missed consensus expectations and management lowered forward guidance because of order pushouts at foundry/logic customers. As we wrote in a previous commentary, given Intel’s capex cut announcement as well as the potential for further export restrictions on ASML’s ability to ship and service products in China, there was a chance the company might have to lower guidance, but the magnitude of the miss seemed to catch some investors offsides. ASML needs €3-4bn of orders in the fourth quarter to hit the midpoint of its revised 2025 guidance, which we believe is achievable. China revenues are normalizing lower (from ~45-50% to ~20% in CY2025), while DUV growth ex-China remains strong. Also, ahead of ASML’s Investor Day in November, the company reiterated 2030 revenue guidance of €44bn - €60bn. While a wide range, the reiteration provided some relief for shares. We believe AI demand remains robust and ASML’s product roadmap is critical to supporting the increased demand for leading-edge semiconductors expected over the coming decade. We remain positive on shares.

Representative Buys (Ticker; Sector)*

Broadcom Inc. (AVGO; Information Technology) – Broadcom Inc. is a global technology company that designs, develops, and supplies a broad range of semiconductor and infrastructure software solutions. The company is the leading provider of custom silicon for AI, an end market we believe is in the early innings of growth with a rapidly expanding SAM (Service Addressable Market). As large cloud customers continue to build out larger and larger clusters, AVGO stands to benefit from the increased complexity through both custom ASICs and a variety of other leading networking solutions. Though lower growth, AVGO’s infrastructure software business (VMWare, acquired in 2023) acts as a cash machine with 70% standalone operating margins. We are attracted to the company’s steady free cash flow generation and disciplined capital allocation, enabled by its dominant market position in a secular growth end market. We believe the current share price does not adequately reflect the longer-term cash flow generation potential of the business.

Tyler Technologies, Inc. (TYL; Information Technology) (1.31%**) – Tyler Technologies provides mission-critical, vertical software solutions for the public sector, primarily state and local governments. Governments have lagged commercial industry in tech modernization and Tyler is a key beneficiary of the accelerating migrations of local and state governments to more modern, SaaS (Software-as-a-Service) and Cloud-based solutions that are finally happening. The company is also following the well understood business model migration path from license-based software to more recurring subscription revenue. Given the company’s focus on vertical market software for state and local governments and schools, the company enjoys a very sticky customer base with low churn and more limited economic sensitivity. Beyond its highly predictive business model, Tyler’s unique and broad platform create sufficient opportunities to continue to generate attractive growth for many years to come. We believe the durability and compounding potential of the company’s revenue, earnings and free cash flow are not fully appreciated.

Representative Sells (Ticker; Sector)*†

The Home Depot, Inc. (HD; Consumer Discretionary) – Home Depot is one of the largest retailers in the U.S. and the world’s largest home improvement specialty retailer. The company operates over 2,300 warehouse-format stores in North America and operates an online business. Home Depot targets the Do-It-Yourself (DIY) and professional services markets with a selection of over 40,000 items. When we first purchased shares over seven years ago, we were attracted to the company’s structural advantages over its leading competitors, including a higher professional services mix, higher ecommerce mix and demographically advantaged store footprint. Though we still believe HD possesses many positive attributes, the macro environment has pressured both growth and margins and our conviction level in the opportunity from here has waned. Accordingly, we have been using the position as a source of cash and recently sold the last of our position to fund ideas we believe provide more upside over the next three-to-five years.

Salesforce, Inc. (CRM; Information Technology) – Salesforce, Inc. provides cloud-based Customer Relationship Management (CRM) technology on top of an extensible and customizable platform. We have owned shares since 2005 and have been trimming shares as the growth algorithm has slowed over the last several years. Though we still believe CRM possesses many positive attributes, we ultimately decided to sell the last of our position to fund other more attractive ideas.

Snowflake Inc. (SNOW; Information Technology) – Snowflake Inc. is an enterprise software company focused on the data cloud. Its cloud-native architecture provides the capabilities of traditional data warehouses, data lakes and other data and analytics platforms while providing infrastructure independence through its multi-cloud support and a pricing model separating storage and compute. This separation allows for better optimization and scalability. SNOW’s architecture also allows for granularly provisioned, live data sharing via public clouds. When we first purchased shares, we were attracted to management’s ability to execute on a sizable and growing addressable market opportunity. Though we still believe SNOW possesses many positive attributes, challenging optimization trends and heightened competition (as AI reshapes the market) have pressured the growth profile of the company, and our conviction level in the opportunity from here has waned. Accordingly, we elected to sell our shares to fund other more attractive ideas.

Market Outlook

As we survey the landscape, there is so much about the current environment that is atypical. Historical analogues continue to prove inadequate. In early 2023, consensus suggested certain recession given an inverted yield curve, contractionary ISM measures, bank failures and a triggering of the so-called Sahm rule (i.e. a 50 bps increase in the unemployment rate). Despite the triggering of these traditionally useful measures, we have yet to register a recession 1. Consensus pivoted in 2024 to a soft or no-landing scenario as inflationary measures declined, and expectations grew for Fed rate cuts. These expectations too have been interrupted by inflation scares, higher long-term interest rates and questions about whether further short-term rate cuts are appropriate. Equity market indices have remained healthy despite these dynamics, but outside the largest index names, performance has signaled less sanguinity. As discussed in prior commentaries, market concentration levels are at the highest levels in decades (top 10 weightings constitute 39% of the S&P 500) and the decoupling of performance between the bigger weightings and others is striking (the equal-weighted S&P 500 underperformed the S&P 500 by ~12% in 2024).

Other anomalies have included the Fed’s 50 basis point rate cut in September, a striking move for a still growing economy with healthy employment trends. After the second longest Fed pause on record, a 4.1% U.S. unemployment rate, and PCE (the Fed’s preferred inflation measure) at 2.1%, the Fed elected to start cutting rates in September 2024. With the S&P 500 near an all-time high, this also marked the highest P/E at the start of a Fed cutting cycle in over 60 years. Also, we have never seen fiscal deficits as high as a percentage of GDP at the beginning of a rate cutting cycle. Current deficit levels have only been reached previously during recessions. We could easily spend the rest of this letter describing what makes the current environment new and different. Our point is simply that there is no clear playbook for the current environment, at least from a top-down perspective.

Yet, as we consider potential paths for both the economy and the market this year, there are many positives to consider. Corporate profits, for one, remain quite healthy. Although we might be a bit skeptical of the amount of acceleration in growth baked into S&P 500 earnings expectations this year, stable to improving outlooks across our portfolio of quality companies is encouraging. Additionally, the potential for several of the new administration’s priorities around reducing regulation and easing some of the heightened scrutiny on mergers and acquisitions should also be favorable tailwinds. While not all proposals will positively impact our portfolio companies (e.g. tariffs), we expect the headwinds to be manageable. Employment trends, balance sheet strength, and recent bookings trends across many of our companies also all indicate positive near-term health.

Balancing the positives, we are also considering what might derail a seemingly Goldilocks scenario priced into the broader market as we enter 2025. One scenario we are closely monitoring is the potential for a second wave of inflation. What if inflation is stubborn and the Fed is not able to cut further, and long-term interest rates continue to increase? Although not our base case, this is a scenario we have contemplated. As we learned in 2022, higher rates can quickly reverse the momentum in stock prices by affecting discount rates, even if fundamentals see limited impact. This new and higher real cost of capital forces the market to differentiate among business models to a much greater extent. If we had to choose between where rates stand today and rates during the ZIRP (Zero Interest Rate Policy) era, we would choose today. Our investment philosophy drives us toward self-funding, asset-light, low-leverage, strong balance sheet, quality businesses with durable advantages that drive market share capture. The current higher cost of capital creates difficulties for lower quality companies in less attractive industries that may be more dependent upon the capital markets for funding. This creates a favorable environment for our high-quality businesses either because of the fundamental opportunities created or the scarcity value that can accrue to businesses with fewer challenges.

Ultimately, as we contemplate the coming year, our eyes are wide open to a range of possibilities. While we are optimistic the current positive trends remain for our companies, we are mindful that negative surprises can create hard pivots in consensus. Our north star remains identifying durably advantaged business models with large, attractive end markets. As long as we do not overpay for the business when we get the business model advantages and market opportunity right, we expect intrinsic value to compound each year at an attractive rate. Multiples may compress and expand from year-to-year skewing the realization of that intrinsic value in any given year, but over time, we believe performance in the portfolio should more closely match the multi-year earnings growth of our portfolio companies and generate attractive returns. While we cannot predict portfolio returns in any given year, we remain highly confident that our portfolio companies will continue to grow their intrinsic value in the coming quarters and years. This focus remains our primary playbook for the current environment.

As always, we remain truly grateful for your trust and support,

Brandon D. Bond, CFA Managing Director Portfolio Manager Equities

Brian M. McNamara Managing Director Portfolio Manager Equities

Bo Fifer, Managing Director Portfolio Manager Equities

* It should not be assumed that an investment in the securities listed was or will be profitable.

**Portfolio securities are stated as a percentage of the Fund’s total net assets including cash and cash equivalents as of December 31, 2024.

† Effective February 28, 2025, the name of the TCW Select Equities Fund changed to the TCW Concentrated Large Cap Growth Fund.

† Not held by the Fund as of December 31, 2024.

1 Historically, a recession has been defined as two consecutive quarters of negative GDP growth. On July 21, 2022, the White House published a blog post titled, “How Do Economists Determine Whether the Economy Is in a Recession?”

Glossary of Terms

Artificial Intelligence (AI) – The theory and development of computer systems able to perform tasks that normally require human intelligence, such as visual perception, speech recognition, decision-making, and translation between languages. Balance Sheet– A financial statement that summarizes a company’s assets, liabilities and shareholders’ equity at a specific point in time. BPS (Basis Points)– A unit that is equal to 1/100th of 1%, and is used to denote the change in a financial instrument. The basis point is commonly used for calculating changes in interest rates, equity indexes and the yield of a fixed-income security. Bullish– An upward trend in the prices of an industry’s stocks or the overall rise in broad market indices, characterized by high investor confidence. Cash Flow– The movement of money into or out of a business, project, or financial product. CPI (Consumer Price Index)– Measures the average change in prices over time that consumers pay for a basket of goods and services. Deficit– The amount by which expenses exceed income or costs outstrip revenues. Expense Ratio– A measure of what it costs an investment company to operate a mutual fund. Fed( Federal Reserve)– The central bank of the United States which regulates the U.S. monetary and financial system. FOMC (Federal Open Market Committee)– The branch of the Federal Reserve Board that determines the direction of monetary policy. Free Cash Flow (FCF)– FCF represents the cash that a company is able to generate after spending the money required to maintain or expand its asset base. GDP (Gross Domestic Product)– The market value of all final goods and services produced within a country in a given period of time. Growth– A diversified portfolio of stocks that has capital appreciation as its primary goal, with little or no dividend payouts. Portfolio companies would mainly consist of companies with above-average growth in earnings that reinvest their earnings into expansion, acquisitions, and/or research and development. Hawkishness– Generally favoring relatively high interest rates in order to keep inflation in check. Inflation– A condition of a rise in the general level of prices of goods and services in an economy over a period of time. Nasdaq– Nasdaq is a global electronic marketplace for buying and selling securities, as well as the benchmark index for U.S. technology stocks. Nasdaq was created by the National Association of Securities Dealers (NASD) to enable investors to trade securities on a computerized, speedy and transparent system, and commenced operations on February 8, 1971. The term “Nasdaq” is also used to refer to the Nasdaq Composite, an index of more than 3,000 stocks listed on the Nasdaq exchange that includes the world’s foremost technology and biotech giants such as Apple, Google, Microsoft, Oracle, Amazon, Intel and Amgen. PCE (Personal Consumption Expenditure)– The primary measure of consumer spending on goods and services in the U.S. economy. Price-to-Earnings Ratio (P/E)– A valuation ratio of a company’s current share price compared to its per-share earnings. Recession– Two consecutive quarters of negative economic growth as measured by a country’s gross domestic product. Return on Investment (ROI)- A performance measure used to evaluate the efficiency of an investment or to compare the efficiency of a number of different investments. Revenue– The value of all sales of goods and services recognized by a company in a period. Russell 1000® Growth Index– Measures the performance of the large-cap growth segment of the U.S. equity universe. It includes those Russell 1000 companies with higher price-to-book ratios and higher expected growth values. Small Capitalization (Small Cap)– Companies with a market capitalization of between $300 million and $2 billion. Soft Landing– A soft landing is the goal of a central bank when it seeks to raise interest rates just enough to stop an economy from overheating and experiencing high inflation, without causing a severe downturn. Standard & Poor’s 500 Index (S&P 500)– An index of 505 stocks issued by 500 large companies with market capitalizations of at least $6.1 billion. It is seen as a leading indicator of U.S. equities and a reflection of the performance of the large-cap universe. Underperform– When an investment is underperforming, it is not keeping pace with other securities or indices. Underweight– A condition where a portfolio does not hold a sufficient amount of a particular security when compared to the security’s weight in the underlying benchmark portfolio. Unemployment Rate– The percentage of the total labor force that is unemployed but actively seeking employment and willing to work. Value– A fund that primarily holds stocks that are deemed to be undervalued in price and that are likely to pay dividends. Volatility– The propensity of the value of an asset or market to rise or fall. Yield Curve– A line that plots the interest rates, at a set point in time, of bonds having equal credit quality, but differing maturity dates. The most frequently reported yield curve compares the three-month, two-year, five-year and 30-year U.S. Treasury debt. This yield curve is used as a benchmark for other debt in the market, such as mortgage rates or bank lending rates. The curve is also used to predict changes in economic output and growth. YoY– Year-over-year. YTD– Year-to-date. ZIRP (Zero Interest Rate Policy)– A policy described by maintaining exceptionally low nominal interest rates used by central banks to stimulate economic growth during periods of prolonged economic slowdowns. The U.S. Federal Reserve Bank (the Fed) implemented ZIRP on December 16, 2008, in response to the global recession triggered by the financial crisis.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Big Retailers Ask China For Help Ahead Of Tariff Announcement
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Walmart (WMT) pushes Chinese suppliers to reduce prices amid Trump tariff fears - report. (00:23) LG Energy reaches deal to buy GM's stake in Michigan battery JV for $2B. (01:56) Visa (V), American Express (AXP) seek to swipe Apple (AAPL) credit card from Mastercard - report. (02:44)

This is an abridged transcript.

Walmart (NYSE:WMT) is reportedly actively pushing its Chinese suppliers to reduce prices in response to tariffs imposed by the Trump administration.

According to a report by Bloomberg that cites people familiar with the matter, the Chinese suppliers are struggling to comply with these demands due to already thin profit margins. Some Chinese manufacturers are having trouble meeting Walmart's (WMT) expectations to reduce costs by up to 10% for each round of tariffs.

The pressure on suppliers has led to varied responses, with some manufacturers concerned that any significant reduction in prices could lead them into financial losses.

A few Chinese factory owners have started to explore alternative manufacturing locations elsewhere in Asia, like Cambodia and Vietnam, to maintain both their long-term relationships with U.S. customers and profitability.

The sources added that Walmart (WMT) and its suppliers are negotiating across product categories, not only by country of origin.

Chinese state media criticized Walmart's (WMT) approach, suggesting that demanding such price cuts could disrupt fair competition and potentially harm both U.S. and Chinese businesses.

The retail giant has been diversifying its supply chain to reduce reliance on China, but the country remains a crucial source for various goods like clothing, toys, and electronics, among others.

It has been widely reported that Costco and Target are also pushing for discounts.

South Korea's LG Energy Solution said Tuesday it agreed to acquire the assets held by its Michigan electric vehicle battery joint venture with General Motors (NYSE:GM) for $2B.

GM (NYSE:GM) has been scaling back its EV plans because of uncertainty about the future of EV battery production and consumer tax credits under the Trump administration, saying in December that it planned to sell its stake in the Michigan plant.

LG said the transaction costs were part of its capital spending plan announced earlier this year, and could be lower than $2B.

The two companies continue to maintain two other U.S. battery plant joint ventures operating in Ohio and Tennessee.

With the acquisition, LG Energy is expected to produce batteries for Toyota (TM), including the supply of 20 GWh of nickel, cobalt, manganese, and aluminum battery modules annually, enough to power ~200K high-performance electric vehicles.

Visa (NYSE:V) has offered Apple (AAPL) ~$100M to switch its popular co-branded credit card to its payment network from Mastercard. (NYSE:MA), according to a media report.

The Wall Street Journal reported on Tuesday, citing people familiar with the matter that American Express (NYSE:AXP) has been seeking the roles of both network provider and the card issuer.

There's already been reporting that JPMorgan Chase (JPM) and Synchrony Financial (SYF) have been competing to replace Goldman Sachs (GS) as the bank issuing the Apple Card. The competition among banks is expected as Goldman (GS) pulls back from consumer finance.

Apple (AAPL), however, is expected to choose a network for its card before it selects the bank to replace Goldman (GS), the WSJ said.

Visa (NYSE:V) is aggressively pitching Apple (AAPL), making an offer similar to the upfront payment it offered when Costco selected a network for its card about 10 years ago. Mastercard (NYSE:MA), though, is fighting to keep its role as the card's network, the same people told the WSJ.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

CoreWeave rockets on third day of trading after tepid IPO response

Big Pharma drops sharply ahead of Trump’s tariff announcement

Wells Fargo warns Tesla could fall 50% after deliveries, earnings, and the Cybercab rollout disappoint

Catalyst watch:

  • Nintendo (OTCPK:NTDOY) is holding a reveal event for the Nintendo Switch 2. A launch of the next-generation console is expected as early as June.

  • Fluor (FLR) will hold a strategy update webcast to discuss the company's strategic plan followed by a live question and answer session.

  • Yext (YEXT) will host an Investor Day event. Management will present the company’s vision, strategy, and financial objectives, followed by a live Q&A.

  • Marex (MRX) will hold an Investor Day event. The event will feature presentations from the company's business heads, providing a comprehensive review of operations and growth initiatives.

  • Shareholders with Nevro (NVRO) will vote on the takeover offer from Globus Medical (GMED).

  • The National Retail Federation will hold a State of Retail & the Consumer 2025 event. BJ's Wholesale Club (BJ) CEO Bob Eddy, Walmart (WMT) U.S. President John Furner, and Tapestry (TPR) CEO Joanne Crevoiserat will be some of the notable speakers.

Now let’s take a look at the markets ahead of the opening bell. Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the red. Crude oil is flat at $71/barrel. Bitcoin is up 0.8% at $85,000. Gold is up 0.5% at $3,100.

In the world markets, the FTSE 100 is down 0.7% and the DAX is down 0.6%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: TTEC Holdings (NASDAQ:TTEC) +34.9% – Shares climbed after the company said it's ready to engage with CEO Kenneth Tuchman on his $6.85 a share proposal.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 8:15 am ADP Employment Report

  • 10:00 am Factory Orders

data.title: China Just Redrew The Aluminum Map, And The World Is Watching
data.description:
Original Post

By Sohrab Darabshaw

At a time when analysts expect the global demand for aluminum to rise, a new development in China may significantly impact both aluminum market demand and the country's internal consumption. That said, market watchers know that any announcement around metals coming out of Beijing these days also has to be looked at through the lens of the ongoing tariff war with the United States.

It's a pivotal moment for China's aluminum industry, even as the nation's production capacity nears its government-imposed annual limit of 45 million tons (MT). The government set that ceiling in order to address overcapacity and reduce emissions. However, it unveiled a fresh plan last week - one that spells out several "ambitious" new goals for the aluminum sector.

Anything that China says or does these days where metals and minerals are concerned makes experts and markets sit up and take note. China is the largest producer and one of the biggest consumers of aluminum, producing about 43 MT annually. As a result, experts and analysts are now scurrying to make sense of the MIIT report and its potential impact on aluminum and bauxite, the latter being the primary source of alumina.

China Intent on Remaining the Global Aluminum Market Leader

While it may be far too early to accurately predict the final outcome of this new plan, initial reactions by a handful of analysts have said that it could be a two-fold attempt by China. They theorize the plan will not only streamline the industry, but allow the country to go "all in" on a greener method of production, thus helping it retain its leadership position in the aluminum market.

Only time will tell how it will pan out, but the anticipated slowdown in China's aluminum production had the industry on edge long before the release of this report. This year, aluminum production in China will likely grow by barely 2%. Next year, some experts predict the figures could be even worse. Meanwhile, the situation remains extremely fluid, especially after the U.S. recently announced the imposition of a 25% tariff on steel and aluminum imports from all other countries, including China.

Salient Features of the New Plan

The new Chinese roadmap stresses better and more eco-friendly output where aluminum is concerned, as well as an improvement of the resilience and security of the aluminum supply chain. According to reports, the blueprint also spelled out targets along the two-year timeline for boosting the robustness of the raw material supply chain, using technology to innovate the production and promoting sustainable production.

The plan also includes proposals to expand processing hubs. This includes boosting energy-efficient electrolytic aluminum capacity to over 30%, increasing clean energy use to at least 30% and enhancing red mud recycling to over 15%.

Technological advancements will likewise focus on breakthroughs in low-carbon smelting and precision processing, driving new growth in aluminum consumption. The goal is to establish a high-quality development framework by 2035, ensuring China remains a global aluminum market leader.

The Bauxite Race

The reality of today's aluminum market is that everybody, including China and the U.S., is looking around for high-quality bauxite ore. For example, China's bauxite imports in the first nine months of 2024 were 20% higher than the previous year. This report, quoting S&P Global, said Beijing's dependence on imported bauxite was about 57%, with most of its supply coming in from Guinea.

However, disruptions in that supply chain, coupled with cutbacks by other suppliers like Australia, has left China and the rest of the world looking for other global suppliers.

An article in Forbes said that in the last two years, the price of Guinea ore increased to US $75/t, a rise of about 50%. Meanwhile, Australian ore price increased 60% at $55/t. Alumina and aluminum prices have also climbed, with the former surging 140% in the past year due to supply disruptions and refinery shutdowns in Australia.

Likely Impact of the New Plan

The slowdown in aluminum production, the deletion of tax rebates on exports and a bevy of other factors are bound to cut down China's export numbers in the coming weeks. This, in turn, will negatively impact aluminum prices. With the announcement of the "aluminum sector makeover" plan, production dynamics will shift as China stresses cleaner production and higher-quality ores.

Under the new plan, China clearly wants to cut reliance on bauxite by boosting recycling, aiming for a 15 MT increase in recycled aluminum output. Much of the plan focuses on improving energy efficiency by investing in tech to cut energy use and lower emissions for this sector.

The opinion is that it's not merely about producing cleaner aluminum, but that China is jockeying to make aluminum more sustainable so it can maintain its global position at the top of the aluminum market.

How Will the Changes Affect the U.S.?

Like China, the U.S. also relies heavily on bauxite imports. Last year, import figures for crude and dried bauxite ore into the U.S. saw a slight drop, though it was higher in the fourth quarter. For its part, the United States mainly imports from countries like Jamaica and Turkey.

With China's new plan, it is important to keep an eye on U.S. aluminum imports from China, Canada and several other countries. Given the ever-changing tariff scenario, the slowing down of China's aluminum sector, the new China plan and other factors, the U.S. may have to start looking for other sources to fill the expected import shortfall.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: United Airlines: The Sell-Off Creates A Unique Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Sometimes fear creates the best opportunities for investors. While buying companies when times are good is usually a good investing strategy, allocating capital when volatility levels and bearish sentiment are high is often when the best value is found.

The airline industry has been hit hard by a number of factors over the last month. In addition to recent economic data weakening, cuts to government agencies are disproportionately impacting the airlines as well, with some major carriers getting nearly 2-3 percent of revenue from the travel of public officials. Oil prices also remain high, and Trump's recent tariffs have created some uncertainty in this sector as well. The Atlanta Fed is now expecting first quarter GDP to fall by 2.8 percent.

One airliner that has been particularly affected by the recent uncertainty is United Airlines Holdings (NASDAQ:UAL).

United Airlines has offered investors total returns of nearly 119 percent over the last 5 years, while the S&P 500 (SPY) has offered investors total returns of 133.5 percent during this same time period. Still, United has also sold off nearly 30 percent in just the last month.

Today, I am initiating coverage of United Airlines Holdings as a buy. The company is very well positioned to benefit from what should be multiple short-term and long-term catalysts moving forward, with the energy prices likely falling and air traffic levels likely to grow in the back half of the year and moving forward for multiple reasons. The stock looks undervalued.

United's fourth-quarter earnings report was very strong. The company stated that earnings per share normalized actual were $3.26, and management reported revenue actual of $14.7 billion. Consensus analyst estimates were for EPS normalized actual of $3.03 a share and revenue actual of nearly $14.35 billion. Management also solid impressive revenue per available seat mile numbers of 18.77, and impressive net margins of 5.52 percent. The company raised guidance for 2025 to $11.50-13.50 a share.

United has nearly 16 percent of the US airline market, and the company's revenue per available seat numbers are stronger than most peers such as Southwest Airlines (LUV) and American Airlines (AAL).

Airline traffic in 2024 exceeded pre-pandemic levels in 2019 and revenue passenger kilometers are expected to grow by 36 percent over the next 5 years by 2030.

United Airlines will likely benefit from a number of upcoming catalysts as well. First, the Trump administration does not believe in climate change, the current leadership is unlikely to impose stringent fuel standards or impose more burdensome regulations on carriers. Second, while the Middle East is obviously often volatile, tensions in this region have fallen. Russia and Ukraine also recently agreed to a cease-fire. Energy prices should fall in the back half of the year as geopolitical tensions ease and new supply comes online from countries such as Papua New Guinea and Brazil. United does not hedge oil prices anymore, and energy is nearly 20-25 percent of the company's overall costs.

United has also been aggressively upgrading the company's fleet. The company increased capacity by nearly 5 percent over the last year, and the carrier is well positioned to benefit from the strong long-term growth in air traffic as people come back to work and the middle class in emerging markets continues to grow.

Air traffic demand is supposed to grow by nearly 35 percent over the next 5 years, according to analysts. United has also largely resolved pilot shortage issues, new labor contracts should be more reasonable.

This is why United looks undervalued. The company currently trades at just 5.59x expected forward GAAP earnings, even though analysts are expecting the carrier to grow earnings at 12-14 percent per year over the next 3 years. Oil prices should also fall significantly if the war between Russia and Ukraine ends, which looks increasingly likely, as some risk premiums come out of the Brent Crude market and new supply comes online in the back half of the year. United also has a very strong balance sheet, with $14.47 billion in cash and mostly longer-term debt. The company will have the flexibility to increase the current modest 1.5 billion share buyback plan.

All investment theses have risks, and the cuts to government agencies will impact United in the short term. Obviously, the economy is likely to worsen in the near term as well. The current cease-fire between Russia and Ukraine is also fragile, and tensions in the Middle East could also flare up again. Oil prices may rise if geopolitical issues aren't controlled. Corporate layoffs and likely cutbacks in business travel will impact the company this year as well.

Still, current risks look to be more than priced into United's stock at current valuation levels. The carrier has a very strong balance sheet, management has a lot of flexibility to navigate the current more challenging operating environment. While the airline industry has underperformed in years past, United is well-positioned for what should be impressive years of growth.

data.title: Americold Realty: Not An Attractive REIT Just Yet
data.description:
I rate Americold Realty Trust, Inc. (NYSE:COLD) a Hold for investors looking for long-term buy and hold investments. Since its IPO in 2018, the industrial real estate investment trust (REIT) price shot higher on enthusiasm for the company, then dropped after Covid, as the company went through many challenges associated with the supply chain disruption.

In 2022, the company hired a new CEO, who quickly changed the senior management team, almost entirely. The result is a company that is stabilizing the business, achieving growth, with the potential to create a world-class real estate warehouse business.

The most important factors that influence my opinion of the REIT are:

  • Mixed Q4 Earnings results show quarterly declines with full-year gains.
  • Complete management changeover in the C Suite
  • Steadily growing dividend, currently paying 4.29% annually.
  • Largest publicly owned cold storage warehouse facilities.
  • Opportunity to build US (18%) and Global (6%) market share.
  • Focus on cold storage as a competitive advantage.
  • Positioned for further mergers and acquisitions to drive growth.

Seeking Alpha Quant rates the stock a Sell 2.35 (as of 4/1/25), which reflects the latest trend showing the REIT dropping in price. The company has a price/FFO ratio of 23.36, which is significantly higher than the Real Estate sector median of 14.26, suggesting that it is priced at a premium.

Americold: A Global Leader in Cold Storage

Based in Atlanta, Georgia, Americold is a global leader in temperature-controlled logistics real estate and value-added services. Focused on the ownership, operation, acquisition, and development of temperature-controlled warehouses, Americold owns and/or operates 239 temperature-controlled warehouses, with approximately 1.4 billion refrigerated cubic feet of storage, in North America, Europe, Asia-Pacific, and South America. Americold’s facilities are an integral component of the supply chain connecting food producers, processors, distributors, and retailers to consumers.

Americold can create custom solutions, catering to their clients specialized needs, such as a “Farm to Fork” experience, which is very popular these days. Management is also focused on acquisition to build growth.

New Management Team

Within the investor deck, COLD highlighted all the new managers working at the company, starting with the CEO who was appointed in 2022. The Chief Legal Officer, Chief Investment Officer and Chief Information Officer started in 2023. The CFO, President, and President of International were all appointed in 2024. In my view, it is odd that so many high level people changed in such a short period of time. This can be seen as an opportunity, such as the new CEO put in his own team, or as a liability, such as they are cleaning up a mess from the previous management group and may not have found all the errors yet.

The COLD investor deck highlights the CEO change along with the associated growth in adjusted funds from operations (AFFO), once he brought in his new team. Changes that drove the growth include focusing on 4 key strategic priorities, building a new partnership with a train line (CPKC), and increasing rent & storage revenues.

This growth in FFO drove earnings and increased margins. Since 2021, COLD grew EBITDA 34% and gross margin from 17.5% to 23.8%.

As the company achieved growth, it also highlights its improved investment grade ratings, which allows them to pursue strategic objectives such as buying new warehouse space.

The new management team is focused on achieving further growth through 4 main objectives:

  1. Reinvesting in the business – maintain buildings, expand capacity, develop partnerships.
  2. Growth through mergers and acquisitions
  3. Maintain healthy financials.
  4. Grow the dividend.

Q4 2024: Mixed Results, However Full Year Was Positive

The overall takeaway from the Q4 earnings call was mixed, making it difficult to conclude the company has sorted out its problems. Fourth Quarter results showed declines in both revenues and earnings, however the full year 2024 showed growth year-on-year.

On February 20, 2025, the company declared Q4 earnings and full year 2024. The financial highlights were as follows:

Q4 Results

  • Decline of -1.9% in total revenues to $666.4 million
  • Net loss of -$36.4 million, or -$0.13 per share
  • Global Warehouse segment same store revenues decreased -0.5%, and margin increased to 13.2% from 6.3%.
  • Adjusted FFO decreased -2.1% to $105.9 million.
  • Core EBITDA decreased -2.9% to $155.6 million.

Full Year 2024

  • 11.4% growth in Global Warehouse same store net operating income (NOI)
  • 16% growth in Adjusted Funds from Operations (AFFO)
  • $125 million increase from same store handling of NOI, i.e., increased profitability.
  • 3 new projects: Kansas City, Dallas Fort Worth, and Sydney, Australia
  • $79 million development in Port Saint John, Canada with partners DP World and CPKC (train lines)

Financial Performance is On Track

From 2021, COLD has grown 40% from $299 million to $$420 million on an adjusted basis, as seen in the chart below. Similarly, net operating income (NOI) has grown +34% over the same time period.

Stock Performance is Mixed at Best

An investment of $10,000 in COLD over the past 5 years, dividends reinvested, would have lost -$2,683, in comparison to the Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ) which would have gained $5,670.

An investment of $10,000 in COLD since going public in 2018, would have grown to $14,890 which just slightly underperforms VNQ.

Valuation

COLD has steadily increased the distribution over time. For 2025, the quarterly dividend increased from $0.22 to $0.23 for the first quarter.

In comparing COLD to peers, as seen in the chart below, the dividend looks attractive at 4.29%, however the 5-year total return is the only industrial REIT that is negative, at -27.9%.

Prologis, Inc. (PLD) – as one of the largest industrial REITs with $105 billion in market capitalization, PLD has an average dividend yield for the sector of 3.61%, one of the lower 5-year total returns at 58.2%.

Rexford Industrial Realty, Inc. (REXR) – offers the highest dividend yield of the group at 4.39%, the highest 5-year dividend growth rate of 16.9%, however one of the lowest 5-year total returns at 8.4%.

First Industrial Realty Trust Inc. (FR) – stands out for its high price to book ratio, suggesting its premium priced, along with one of the lowest dividend yields at 3.3%.

STAG Industrial Inc. (STAG) – has one of the highest dividend yields at 4.13%, with the highest 5-year total return at 98.5%.

Terreno Realty (TRNO) – has a decent 5-year dividend growth rate of 12.8%, and a 5-year total return of 39%.

Outlook

The future of COLD appears compelling, however some of the current metrics around the stock are unattractive. COLD has the opportunity to grow through building new spaces, partnering with clients to build custom warehouses, or mergers and acquisitions.

On the Q4 Earnings call, the company announced a $34 million expansion project in Christchurch, New Zealand, dedicated to the country’s largest grocery.

Risks To Rating

The main risk for industrial properties such as COLD is macroeconomic, such as rising unemployment and recession. Although they use long-term leases, a serious recession could force some companies to reduce their inventories, hence have a lower demand for industrial space.

As of March 19th, the Federal Reserve is on hold for interest rates, but it is widely believed that the Fed will cut rates twice to three times in 2025. Further cuts in rates typically help REITs as it lowers the cost of borrowing for both the landlord and the tenant that is warehousing product.

Bottom Line

In trying to decide what to do with this investment, there is a bull case and a serious bear case to be made with the conflicting numbers being analyzed. Therefore, I rate Americold Realty Trust, Inc. a Hold. Full disclosure, I don’t own this REIT and don’t plan to buy shares any time soon. There are far more compelling REITs that either generate more income and or can build capital appreciation. In my opinion, COLD is overpriced. The CEO makes a good case in the investor pitch deck, but throughout its P&L shows metrics that cause more concern than confidence.

The bull case can be made by observing the relatively new CEO pointing to growth metrics driven by his new team in place. The dividend has been steadily growing since the IPO, and indeed, it was raised by one cent going into 2025. As the largest publicly owned cold storage facility, the opportunity to grow is wide. COLD has only 18% market share in the U.S. and only 6% globally. Future building or growth through mergers and acquisitions can supply new warehouse space and revenue if managed efficiently.

The bear case is history makes the REIT look very unattractive. Even within the last 5 years, it hasn’t been a good security to own. Looking forward, there may be other REITs that are more compelling.

data.title: Chubb: A Bright Future Underpinned By Top-Tier Underwriting
data.description:
The $120B market cap insurer Chubb (NYSE:CB) ended 2024 with over $62B in gross written premiums. We know where those premiums were written, but we don't know how profitable they will turn out to be.

That is, in a nutshell, the insurance business. You get paid first, then you learn the cost. Writing profitable insurance policies is both an art and a science. Underwriters need to assess both the likelihood and severity of losses, sometimes aided by engineering support, to gather estimates and asset-specific risk conditions.

The statistics help, especially in commoditized segments like auto, home, life, but for specialty insurance, the underwriter's "nose" the art, is even more important.

On the customer's side, the act of buying insurance is an act of trust. Money goes out the door on day 1. All you get is a promise that things will be put back the way they were just before risk happened to you.

So, a pristine reputation for making good on such promises is of paramount importance; equally important is making the right promises and avoiding the wrong ones. Alas, we don't know how the current set of promises will turn out to be. The best we can do is look at how good (or bad) they have done in the past.

Where Premium Comes From

Chubb is a globally diversified property and casualty ("P&C") insurer. Their premium breakdown by geography is:

Diversification is important for any company, but more so for insurance. Their global footprint means that risk is spread out better, minimizing the probability that one catastrophe ends up permanently damaging the company.

Such wide diversification is not unusual for industry participants that have a focus on re-insurance (most of them add a "Re" to their name), where whole categories of risk are offloaded by insurers originating the policies. Geographic diversification is required for survival in re-insurance.

But Chubb is an insurer, meaning it is the "face" in front of clients in all these geographies, and they also retain a large amount of their risks. What this means is that Chubb is a "straightforward" insurance operation, their large footprint is a luxury, not a requirement. On the other hand, they stand to benefit or suffer directly from the outcome of their own underwriting decisions - a condition that demands greater focus.

Focused Execution

The way Chubb achieved their global reach is open to others - mainly through acquisitions. It has certain scale benefits, but is not the main differentiating factor. What really is special and points to very disciplined execution is their underwriting results.

This is where it all starts - or ends - in insurance. If you can't underwrite profitably, you won't be doing it for long. On the other hand, if you can - and excel at it - there is a lollapalooza of knock-on effects that can create outsized results.

Their underwriting track record speaks for itself:

2017 and 2020 were big cat years. Still, Chubb came out with black ink on both occasions. As a remainder, any amount under 100% means profits, and the lower the combined ratio, the better. The industry peers, as a group, have a ten-year average ratio of 98%, meaning that only $0.02 of every $1 in premiums collected made it to net income. Even such a disappointing result is better than most periods, when the industry tends to operate at 100% or even slightly above.

Consistently profitable underwriting means you get to keep more of the money to invest in your business. It also means regulators like you better and rating agencies grant you the very best ratings available (A++, from AM Best, for Chubb). Which translates into clients and brokers feeling better about your promises too.

Even better, you get to keep more of your float.

The Float

As of December 2024, CB has more than $110B in float. These are liabilities, consisting of expected and incurred losses that mean money will go out to policyholders. But not just yet.

Because premiums recur, and money is fungible, this float usually remains constant. Settled claims from last year can be paid from money received this yesterday. As premiums grow so does float, and float can be invested for the benefit of shareholders.

Combine float with some other liabilities (very little debt of $15B) and equity capital, and you get an investment portfolio of over $150B. That, divided by 405 million shares, is $370/share (the stock trades at $300).

This investment portfolio is overwhelmingly comprised of fixed income and investment grade securities - to preserve liquidity and happy regulators.

Thus, the value of float is directly proportional to the level of interest rates. As rates started to increase, so has investment income, which reached $6B in 2024, up from $3.5B three years ago.

The longer rates remain elevated, the more bonds that mature get reinvested at higher coupons. This incremental revenue requires not a single additional employee or computer, it is simply a consequence of the removal of the Fed's heavy hand on the short end of the yield curve. Zero rate policies were punishing savers, and some of the biggest savers are insurance companies.

We seem to be a long way away from ZIRP. Even if we face a recession that requires easing, they are unlikely to stay low for long. The cost of doing that again is now too real.

Aside from low-risk bonds, Chubb has taken a moderate portion of their portfolio (about 20%) and gone all-out on private equity and private credit. These investments are appropriate as long as the regulated insurance entities have all covered their liquidity needs, and the extra yield provides a boost to shareholder returns.

Putting It All Together

For the decade leading up to 2022, all P&C insurers were operating at half throttle. They had to rely on their underwriting operation for profits, very little was coming from the investment activities. During 2010-2020, ROIC and ROE were pressed lower despite consistently profitable underwriting.

This has now changed, and better returns are on the horizon:

Combining one of the most consistently profitable underwriters with a much friendlier investment return potential bodes well for Chubb shareholders. Keep in mind that profitable float is equal to negative-cost of funding. A very happy condition most shoot for, but few actually achieve.

As always, no matter how good the track record, valuation matters.

Not a Chubby Price

The current P/E is a little below its historical average of 15. I use 2016 as the base year because that is when ACE merged with Chubb and the combined company was created.

When considering the current P/E, remember that we should be looking forward. We are capitalizing future earnings, and future earnings will be supported by higher investment returns. If that is so, a slight premium to average would be more appropriate.

The dividend yield, at a conservative 1.2%, is sure to grow. As it has for many years. Returns should not be viewed as dependent on dividends. Now that acquisitions have slowed, the company has focused on returning cash via share buybacks.

For the past four years, cumulative repurchases have exceeded $12B. About 10% of the current market cap. Just considering, the dividend misses more than half of the return.

Yearly cash used for share repurchases ($ Billions):

Should there be any doubt about these repurchases being carried out at sensible valuations, there is some hope. Warren Buffett was also buying shares at the same time.

Risk

The CEO, Evan Greenberg, has been key in orchestrating the company's transformative journey for the last decade. But he is 70 now. There is always the chance he wishes to remain as CEO or as Chairman, of course. And by now, the underwriting discipline is deeply engrained in the company's culture - I expect. But as for corporate strategy, his contributions are hard to overstate.

Some new classes of risk have yet to really mature. Cyber risks is one area where cascading losses may not be fully covered by reinsurance. This is a relatively new area in a very conventional market, and only time actual loss experience will end up molding the right policy wording.

If, for whatever reason, we return to a period of very low interest rates that lasts for many years, returns will certainly suffer (again). I regard this as a very low probability scenario, but nothing can be fully discarded in economics.

Takeaway

Not all insurance companies earn similar returns. Some achieve greatness by gaining unusual prowess on the investment side (i.e. Berkshire Hathaway), others, like Chubb, earn more than their share by focusing on great underwriting - which is always the necessary condition to anything else.

Going forward, the company should continue to grow. Insurance is structurally tied to economic growth, and resilient even during recessions. Profitable insurers can grow faster, as their own success allows for higher ratings, higher investment in customer service and winning loyal customers.

I often think large insurers look somewhat like banks. The main assets are a bunch of corporate loans (bonds) funded by other people's money. Going forward, ROE should return to a more normal 14% to 16%, aided by higher re-investment rates. This is about similar to what can be expected from most large banks, for example, but with much less structural risk. You cannot have a run on a float.

data.title: I'm All In On Evolution AB: Stock Price May Shoot Up Following U.S. Expansion
data.description:
Intro

My investment process is to buy outstanding companies at a reasonable price and then hold them in my portfolio for years, if not decades. However, the stock market is unpredictable, so when I buy a company, I never go all in right away. Typically, I consider 3-4 decreasing price entries, and with Evolution AB (OTCPK:EVVTY) I have just reached the last threshold. I invested everything I wanted to invest, and I am satisfied that I created such a large position. I will hardly buy again unless the price suffers a significant collapse (-20%/-30%) for no relevant reason.

It is quite rare for me to go all in on a company, usually the price collapse stops before my 3-4 tranche. The last time I happened to invest so much was with Meta (META), under $100 per share: I was all in and did not expect the collapse to be so pronounced. The hope is that Evolution will turn out to be Meta 2.0, but of course, every investment is unique and comparisons are not very helpful.

I personally started buying around $120 per share, and now that I see the stock at $74 per share I am tempted to invest much more, but I must restrain myself. The reason I am so bullish on Evolution is that it is one of a kind; find me another company with the following characteristics:

  • World leader in its market with 60-80% market share in Europe, 90%+ in North America.
  • Profit margin of 60%, higher than Nvidia (NVDA) and Visa (V), two of the most profitable American companies.
  • Net cash of $733 million, so it has more cash than debt.
  • Issues growing dividends and does buybacks.
  • Revenues are growing in double digits and there are major growth catalysts.
  • PE Ratio is 11.50x, Price to Free Cash Flow is only 12.50x.

This company reflects all my requirements to make it one of the largest positions in my portfolio. It is obviously not risk-free, but at these prices I think the downside is limited compared to the upside.

Why its competitive advantage is so wide

Evolution's market share in live casinos is dominant, and there is little doubt about that.

In the first four categories, i.e., where Evolution operates, the latter is the top game provider in the world. In terms of Best Game Shows, i.e., the strongest point of this company, with the exception of Australia, Evolution leads in all geographic areas. In Canada, there is nothing but Evolution, and the only country it has not yet dominated is the U.S., but in the next few paragraphs I will show you why this is the main catalyst for its growth.

At this point, you might wonder why Evolution is so dominant, after all it only creates online games where people bet money; it doesn't make 2nm semiconductors, and it doesn't even own the world's leading payment system. The secret of its success lies in two factors:

  • The first is to have an outstanding CPO (Chief Product Officer) who makes Evolution's corporate vision different from its competitors. CPO Todd Haushalter, in a 2018 interview, revealed that Evolution's goal was not to compete with other online casino games providers, but with Netflix and YouTube. This statement may seem out of place at first glance, but it actually makes perfect sense. Evolution's main purpose is to entertain people through its games, to make them feel part of something and, most importantly, to entertain them through talented hosts. Of course, people will spend money while they stay glued to the screen, and that's where Evolution's revenue will come from. In a way, it's like watching your favorite TV game show and being able to bet on the right answers: you can be the contestants at any time and talk to other players. Now this situation may seem absurd to us, but in time I believe this process will normalize.
  • The second factor that makes Evolution dominant is the scale of its business. To make its business profitable, billions of dollars must be invested to create the infrastructure and attract top talent; that is why land-based casinos do not have online tables as well. You might think, why don't they use some rooms to create online tables and enter this market? The answer is that they would only lose money because it is complicated to reach the break-even point in this market. Currently, Evolution is so profitable because it runs 1700+ online tables and has trained thousands of presenters to make its games more entertaining. It is not easy to find so much talent, train them, and not let them get away. For this reason, not only land-based casinos but also online providers cannot keep up with Evolution. The scale of its business is too large, and you also must overcome the regulatory problems I will tell you about later.

Overall, Evolution's competitive advantage can be summed up in a few words:

  • It takes years and years of success to build a portfolio of games like Evolution. However, being the most creative company in this area, each year the gap with competitors grows larger and larger.

  • It has the best talent and the infrastructure to support them and expand. Its business is heavily skewed toward fixed costs, resulting in huge margins but at the same time making it very difficult for new competitors to reach the break-even point: hundreds and hundreds of continuous plays are required.

Finally, another aspect that makes Evolution's business exceptional is that it has no direct contact with players. It licenses its games to online gaming providers such as 888 casino and William Hill: from them, it receives a fixed commission and in some cases a variable one based on the total amount played on its games. Thus, Evolution is not in direct competition with casino games providers; rather, they are its customers. The only competitors currently are Playtech and Pragmatic Play, but both do not have a market share comparable to Evolution (less than half) in live casino games. Among other things, they do not have the same scalability, in fact, the profit margins are not comparable.

Why the price per share keeps falling

At this point, you might ask: but if Evolution is such a dominant and profitable company, why does its price per share continue to fall? The reasons, in my view, are mainly three, and none of them is a long-term problem.

It is a Swedish company in a controversial market

Being a Swedish company is not a disadvantage per se, but it implies less attention from the market. If it were listed on NYSE it would be different and many investors I think would not miss Evolution at these prices. Being listed only in Stockholm is a limitation in this respect, and I hope the company will consider dual listing in the future. Also, it certainly does not help to be a sin stock, as many people/funds do not invest in this type of company.

This is not directly related to Evolution's collapse, but I think it is one of the reasons why there has not yet been a reversal of the trend. More media attention is needed on this company, since many people still do not know about it. An American company with these fundamentals would not have lasted very long, with a PE Ratio of just 11.50x.

Cyber-attacks and fraud in the Asian region

Compared to a few years ago, Evolution has become a behemoth and its revenue growth has had to adjust accordingly: it is not possible to grow 50% per year all the time.

As you can see from this chart, over the past year, revenues have increased by 14.68%, which is a big figure, but for the market it was not enough. Undoubtedly, Evolution's growth rate has slowed down a lot in recent years, but is it so bad to grow 15% per year with a 60% profit margin? Does this company really deserve a PE Ratio of 11.50x?

In addition to the normalization of revenue growth as a result of increased size, there is another factor that is weighing negatively: the ongoing cyber-attacks in Asia.

Asia is the geographic area that generates the most operating revenue, but since the beginning of 2024 it has experienced an abrupt stall in growth. The point is not that Evolution's games are no longer appreciated in Asia, but there are illegal websites that stream its games without paying any commission. Certainly, online piracy is a problem, but the company is working to solve it:

We will see issues with the cyber-attacks hijacking our product and we estimate to solve them within a couple of quarters. We will continue to deploy full force in countermeasures and we are implementing changes that make it more and more difficult to take advantage of our system in an unintended way. I reiterate that as we stated in Q3, it will take a few quarters before we have come to terms with the problem and we are in that process right now.

CEO Martin Carlesund

Online piracy will never be completely eradicated (Netflix docket), but reducing it would already make a big difference. Believing that Evolution can no longer grow in Asia for this reason seems too shallow to me. I expect that in a few quarters there will be improvements and the stock will unlock its growth in the most important geographic area in terms of operating revenues.

Problems with UK authorities

In late 2024, the UK Gambling Commission initiated a review of Evolution's operating license in the UK after it was discovered that its games are being supplied to unlicensed operators. What are the consequences if Evolution is guilty? It could lose its license in the UK, be fined, or have no sanction at all. In the worst case, i.e., revocation of the UK license, Evolution would no longer be able to operate in this country, but this is not a huge damage since it generates only 3% of revenues.

The market has discounted in the current price that this 3% is already lost, but this is not the case at all:

  • First of all, Evolution's fault must be verified, and I have my doubts about that. A company that generates billions a year I don't think has any interest in generating small revenue on the black market, so Evolution may be a victim rather than the perpetrator. The company is willing to cooperate right now with the UK Gambling Commission in order to clarify the matter.
  • Second, even if Evolution were to be guilty, the chance of the license being revoked from the undisputed leader in live show casino games is very low. How do I know? I went to the UK Gambling Commission's website and saw all the actions it has taken toward corporations that have committed wrongdoing from August 25, 2022 to the present. Out of 63 total cases, only 2 unknown companies had their licenses revoked, in most cases, there were warnings or financial penalties. Personally, in the worst-case scenario, I believe Evolution will have to pay a fine, but it will be minimal compared to all the money it generates. At most a few million.

In 2023, 32Red, one of the leading online casinos in the UK (among other things, one of Evolution's clients) had to pay a fine of £4.19 million for significantly heavier charges: money laundering, terrorist financing, and failure to comply with the code of social responsibility. How high could Evolution's fine be? The highest imposed by the UK Gambling Commission was £6 million on Gamesys Operations. Even if it were £10 million, Evolution would not even notice since it generated a net income of €1.24 billion in 2024.

Growth catalysts

We have just seen what is bringing down Evolution's revenues, but now let's talk about the catalysts that could restore confidence in this stock.

First of all, Evolution exploits an underlying trend in its favor, which is the gradual adoption of live game casinos instead of land-based ones. Digitization has not only changed the way we shop or have conversations, but also the way we bet. Among other things, live game casinos offer a higher RTP than land-based ones since they have lower operating costs, which means the player has a better chance of winning in the short term.

Currently, only 13% of Evolution's revenues come from the North America segment, but given its popularity in Canada, the U.S. is likely to weigh in at a few percentage points. This implies a huge growth opportunity for Evolution because to date, it has done without one of the most prosperous gambling markets in the world.

The U.S.'s commercial gambling industry generated a new record revenue in 2024 for the fourth year in a row, $71.92 billion. Compared to the total, $8.41 billion came from online casinos in the seven states where this activity is regulated.

Interest is at an all-time high. In fact, in January 2025, iGaming revenue (Evolution's category) saw a 34.70% increase over last year. This segment is growing much faster than traditional casino and sports betting. People increasingly like its live games, and if the iGaming industry were to be regulated in other states as well, Evolution's potential would be huge. To date, only seven states have allowed iGaming, but experts believe it is only a matter of time before others follow suit.

Even if all states allow iGaming, it will not be easy for Evolution to fully enter this market. Endless paperwork is required to operate in each state, but I believe Evolution will eventually succeed and expand its competitive advantage over companies that do not receive a license. From a certain point of view, the acquisition of the American Galaxy Games was for just that: to get more licenses in the US.

Once it enters a thriving market like the United States, it will be quite easy for Evolution to succeed in gaining market share. Its games are appreciated all over the world and apparently in the seven U.S. states as well. CPO Todd Haushalter's ideas are many for profiting from the U.S. market, and I attach an interesting interview about it:

Ali (interviewer): I feel like Evolution holds a golden ticket with Crazy Time in the US. It is Evolution's biggest hit even after many years, and it should present a huge opportunity in the US market. Am I being too optimistic?

Todd: Obviously, opening a new Crazy Time in every state is a hassle, but we have great initial results.

Ali: Is Crazy Time's international success transferable to the US?

Todd: It is certainly possible. As an example, Lightening Roulette is already very popular in the US, partly surpassing our regular roulette.

Ali: Here's my pitch: Why not invest in creating a top-notch (and possibly long-term) presenter team for the Crazy Time in one of the US states, and then stream internationally from there? In addition to their native language advantage, Americans tend to make great entertainers.

Todd: It is an interesting idea. Either way, it will need some time. If you compare today, our Latvia-based presenters are performing very well, and our US presenters are still to catch them. In a similar vein, we even consider celebrity appearances in our games. Theoretically, if Taylor Swift visits us and turns the wheel in Crazy Time, we probably couldn't handle the demand in our network.

Ali: Sounds like a good problem to have.

As mentioned at the beginning, Todd Haushalter sees Evolution as an entertainment company, and we cannot rule out some Hollywood star turning the Crazy Time wheel in the future. There are so many ideas still unexpressed and Evolution's potential is yet to be discovered. If the new states regulate iGaming, Evolution's growth could approach the same levels as in the past and the profit margins would remain high.

We currently don't do it but have the capacity to stream even from the US studios internationally. The higher wages wouldn't matter if the incremental user is coming at no extra cost.

CEO Carlesund

The high scalability of the business would also be able to support the increase in wages. Obviously, U.S. employees require higher wages than Georgians, but this would not be a problem given the potential revenue.

Overall, interest from traditional casinos to online casinos is the catalyst for revenues in the long term, but in the short to medium term, expansion into the United States may be the turning point. This is a thriving market that Evolution has yet to exploit to its full potential. If other states start regulating iGaming, Evolution's revenues will skyrocket as they have in the past.

In any case, this does not mean that the company will not grow without the United States. Despite all the momentary difficulties in Asia, operating revenues are up 15% YoY and 2025 is a year full of new releases. By the way, according to the CPO, Evolution is working on its biggest game ever, but it will be released in 2026:

We are currently developing our biggest game yet. We are planning to release it next year. It will have a light-hearted theme like Crazy Time and mass appeal.

Fair value and risks

Now comes the fun part, which is trying to figure out how much Evolution is worth. Typically, I enter a growth and discount rate into the DCF model and calculate the fair value, but now I want to follow a reverse process. With a discount rate of 12% (higher than the historical annual average of the S&P500) what is the growth starting from FCF 2024 (in dollars) that Evolution must sustain over the next 10 years to justify the current price?

Practically speaking, Evolution would have to grow 4% per year to justify its current price, and would still yield 12% per year. Personally, I find it unreasonable to think that it could grow so slowly with these growth catalysts, which is why I consider Evolution to be one of the best opportunities available right now. With a reasonable growth rate of 15%, the fair value would rise to $141 per share, almost 100% from current levels.

This company has reached a ridiculous level and that's why I just went all in. If it were to drop another 30% I might consider increasing my position, but I don't think it will be necessary: with these growth rates the PE Ratio will soon reach single digits. How long can this negative sentiment towards such a company last? Its earnings continue to rise and sooner or later, it will reach its fair value.

If there is strong expansion in the USA over the next 10 years, I wouldn't rule out a growth rate of 20% per year. At that point, the fair value would be $191 per share, 156% more than today and with a discount rate of 12%, which is quite high. With these fundamentals, I believe Evolution's downside risk is very low compared to the upside, so all the conditions are in place for it to be a strong buy.

Obviously, there are risks associated with the investment, and they should not be underestimated. It's not so much a question of competition, but of regulations. Only 41% of Evolution's revenues depend on regulated markets, so a huge chunk of its revenues come from unregulated markets. The latter are not illegal, it's just that the legislator is neither for nor against iGaming. This means that in the future there could be regulations to follow, and they could impact Evolution's revenues. Following the rules means increasing operating costs, and this doesn't benefit Evolution.

If the majority of revenues came from regulated markets, the quality of its revenues would increase considerably, but margins could fall a little; a good compromise after all. Massive regulation of iGaming in the USA could be the right catalyst for revenues from unregulated markets to become the minority. However, this is not the case today and it must be taken into account.

Finally, in such a controversial market, you must get used to the constant sanctions that the company will suffer. As effective as Evolution may be in its controls, there will always be a reason that can lead to a legal dispute, as is happening today in the UK. It's part of the business, and you have to be prepared to continuously bear this risk: there will never be a time when Evolution is not a sin stock.
