(1) LVS Event-Driven was incepted on January 1, 2019. LVS Growth was incepted on January 1, 2020.

Dear Investors,

For the first quarter of 2025, the LVS Event-Driven Portfolio gained 0.8% and the LVS Growth Portfolio gained 0.3%, net of all fees and expenses. This compares to the high-yield bond index which gained 0.9% and the S&P 500 (SP500, SPX) which declined 4.3%, our benchmarks respectively.

While I tend to discuss stocks we purchased in our investment letters, I wanted to flip the script and discuss some stocks we recently sold to highlight our approach to risk management.

Event-Driven Portfolio: Selling Howard Hughes

Howard Hughes Corp (HHH) is a real estate holding company that owns several master planned communities (“MPCs”) across the United States. Master planned communities are large-scale residential developments designed to integrate commercial spaces (stores, offices) and recreational facilities (parks, golf courses). In other words, MPCs are mini-cities within cities that cater to upper-middle class families. The flagship MPC in Howard Hughes’s portfolio is The Woodlands located in Houston, Texas.

I became interested in Howard Hughes last summer after the company divested the South Street Seaport. The Seaport had been a disaster project for the company, incinerating cash, and made the rest of the business difficult for analysts to value. After conducting research on the remaining assets, we learned that the MPC portfolio consisted of high-quality real estate assets and we believed that the stock implied a significant discount to the net asset value. Furthermore, the stock was poised to be re-valued higher as the financials improve post-Seaport spin- off. We purchased shares of Howard Hughes in August 2024 for ~$74.55 per share.

Around the same time we became Howard Hughes shareholders, Bill Ackman announced his interest in potentially acquiring the rest of the company he didn’t own. I viewed Ackman’s involvement as win/win. If Ackman wanted to pay fair value for the company, we could earn a quick 30%+ return. If Ackman withdrew his offer, we would be left with a discounted stock that could still be catalyzed higher by the Seaport spin-off.

After several months of radio silence, Ackman came back with an offer in January 2025 to buy a minority interest in the company and then take control of the management team and board, making himself the Chairman and CEO. The puzzling thing about the offer was that not only would Howard Hughes stay public but Ackman’s hedge fund would charge 1.5% management fees based on the size of the market cap as compensation for reinvesting the company’s cash. I viewed this as a non-starter that would surely get rejected by the Board and potentially the starting point towards negotiating a full take private.

Unfortunately, Ackman came back in February 2025 with an even worse offer. Instead of buying out some minority shareholders at a premium, he was now offering to infuse cash directly into the company, take control of the board and management, and pay his hedge fund the 1.5% management fee based on the market cap. What really broke my resolve to hold on for a better offer was that Ackman was now threatening to force this deal upon the company without a shareholder vote. Given Ackman’s outsized ownership in the stock and history of activism, it now started to feel like Ackman would get his way even if it was to the detriment of the rest of the investor base. We sold our shares of Howard Hughes in February 2025 for ~$76.80 per share for a slight profit over what we initially paid.

It is quite possible that Bill Ackman will transform Howard Hughes into a successful investment firm that gets diversified beyond real estate. However, this was not what we signed up for when we bought the stock.

Furthermore, Ackman is not ingratiating himself by taking control of the company without a vote or paying existing shareholders. Clearly, he is only looking out for himself.

The bottom line is that when our thesis changes or we can no longer trust the leadership of the company, we will immediately sell our position and move on.

Growth Portfolio: Selling Icon Plc

Icon Plc (ICLR) is a contract research organization (“CRO”). In the Q3 2024 letter, I highlighted our investment in Medpace (MEDP) another CRO that directly competes with Icon. I became interested in Icon because we liked the CRO business model (after learning about Medpace) and felt that the industry would benefit from long-term tailwinds. However, Icon has taken a different approach than Medpace.

Medpace is focused on serving small biotech companies with full service CRO work (read our detailed Medpace write-up for more context). On the other hand, Icon is more focused on serving larger pharma companies and has pursued an aggressive M&A strategy to roll-up the industry. For example, Icon acquired PRA Health Sciences for $12 billion in 2021. Therefore, we felt that Icon and Medpace were sufficiently different bets that were worth making. We purchased shares of Icon in March 2024 for ~$323.00 per share.

Initially we were quite impressed with Icon’s financial track record. From 2014 to 2022, Icon grew revenue by 17.8% per year and grew EBITDA by 18.5% per year. Furthermore, the stock was available at a mid-teens EBIT multiple, which we felt was attractive. However, we found some cracks in the story as we dug deeper into our research.

The biggest issue we encountered was a lack of evidence supporting the synergies claimed from Icon’s M&A activity. Our conversations with industry experts and CRO customers revealed that M&A actually hurt Icon because it resulted in disruptions in customer projects due to excessive staff turnover and corporate reorganization. Icon had also racked up a lot of debt which it had to work through during a period of rising interest rates.

Ultimately, our research on Medpace revealed that serving small biotech companies was simply a better model than working with large pharma as a CRO. We ultimately decided to sell our shares in Icon in July 2024 for ~$334.53 per share (taking a small profit) and used the proceeds to double down on our investment in Medpace.

The sale of Icon’s stock was well-timed. Icon’s stock has since declined by over 40%. Our investment in Medpace has not been a home run (yet) but has fared much better.

The bottom line is that when our research reveals flaws in an investment thesis, we will quickly sell our position and move on. And because we don’t always get it right with our initial thesis, we must extensively research and validate our beliefs to avoid costly mistakes.

Last month LVS Advisory welcomed Daniel Yuan as an investment analyst supporting Luis. Daniel graduated from New York University’s Stern School of Business in 2024 and spent the past year as an investment analyst at a family office. Daniel will be working from our legacy “NYC office”.

data.title: Weis Markets: Significant Earnings Growth In Q4 Deserves A Rating Upgrade
data.description:
Intro

We wrote about Weis Markets, Inc. (NYSE:WMK) in September of last year after the company's Q2 earnings release, when we reiterated our 'Hold' rating on the Pennsylvanian supermarket retailer. Although the company was dealing with some growth issues at the time, we did point out how its technicals were changing for the better, due in part to Weis' continuous cash-flow performance, leading to growing book value over time.

Remember, the technical chart (participating investors of Weis) is at all times trying to predict where the stock will be trading in 12 to 18 months. Suffice it to say, (Given the technicals always surmise the resulting fundamentals of the stock), it was not surprising to see GAAP profit increase by 11% in Q3 and by a very impressive 69% in Q4 (coming in at $34.7 million for the quarter). The release of Weis Markets' Q4 & annual report in late February this year added to year-to-date gains, which see shares having risen close to 16% already this year.

What is fundamental however is that (due to the excellent performance from Weis Markets over the past two quarters), we now have a clear MACD buy signal on the long-term monthly chart, which is key. This indicator is especially key on longer-term charts due to the dual role of the indicator and the fact that a significant amount of information goes into the forming of the crossovers. Furthermore, for extra confirmation that Weis Markets may be in the initial innings of a sustained long-term bull run, we also have a long-term golden cross in play (the 10-month moving average has moved above its 40-month counterpart). This alone should bring momentum players onto the long side in this play before long.

Return on Incremental Capital

Therefore, building on the points raised earlier concerning how the technicals have been pricing in gains for WMK for some time now, all this comes down to the company's sustained earnings power even in negative top-line growth environments. For example, the company is just after investing over $161 million into its capital expenditure budget in fiscal 2024, all of which was covered by its fiscal 2024 operating cash flow of $187.5 million.

Therefore, we see the overall 5.9% reported annual EPS growth rate in fiscal 2024 gaining traction as earnings growth stems from Weis' return on incremented capital. Despite recent momentum in Q3 & especially Q4, if we take a 6% bottom-line growth projection for fiscal 2025, we can state the following concerning Weis Markets' ROIC.

Fiscal 2024 Earnings $4.09
Expected Fiscal 2025 Earnings $4.34
Additional EPS Earned $0.25
Trailing Dividends Per Share $1.36
Additional Capital $2.73

So by theoretically generating $0.25 from a capital base of $2.73 per share in fiscal 2025, we can state that our forward-looking ROIC in WMK (Assuming 6% bottom-line growth) comes in at 9.16%. This readout is a far cry from the company's stated ROC of 5.18%, demonstrating that returns are comfortably beating the company's cost of capital.

Strong Cash & ST Position

Furthermore, Weis Markets remains a sound investment in the retailer space because shares are trading for under 6 times their Cash & ST position ($382 million). To put this into perspective, approximately half of the above total is in cash while the remainder is invested in corporate & municipal bonds as well as equity securities which continue to generate solid returns for the retailer. In fiscal 2024, interest & investment income amounted to an impressive $22 million (17% of annual EBIT)

Therefore, if the Trump administration's policy on tariffs were to result in increased costs or accelerating inflation, the diversity & size of Weis Markets' Marketable Securities you feel would act as an excellent hedge to keep the company's profitability metrics ticking over.

Attractive Valuation Using Dividend Discount Model

Since Weis Markets has been paying a dividend for approximately 35 years, we can use the formula below to try and stamp an accurate valuation on the stock post the company's fiscal 2024 earnings

EDPS denotes the company's expected dividends per share per year. Since management has kept the quarterly payout at about $0.34 for some time now, we will maintain EDPS at $1.36 in our calculation.

CCE equates to the company's cost of equity. It is a key metric as it expresses the desired return from shareholders in exchange for their capital. We calculate CCE by means of the following formula where we use the 10-year US treasury bond (4.16%) as our risk-free rate, beta (0.52), and a third-party calculation for 'Equity Risk Premium' (4.33%)

Cost of equity = Risk-Free Rate + (Beta) x (Equity Risk Premium) = 6.41%

DGR represents Weis Markets' theoretical dividend growth rate (what the company could afford to increase the dividend annually). We calculate DGR by multiplying the company's GAAP retention ratio (67%) by ROE (7.8%). DGR works out to be 5.23%.

Therefore, when we plug in all of the calculated values into our original formula above, we get

Value of Weis Markets = $1.36 / 0.0641 - 0.0523 = $115.25 per share.

Conclusion

Therefore, to sum up, we are upgrading our rating in Weis Markets to a 'Buy' due to an excellent fourth-quarter earnings report, growing return on capital numbers, and a projected valuation well north of the prevailing share price. Furthermore, the retailer looks nicely hedged against rising costs due to its large short-term investment positions. We look forward to continued coverage.

data.title: Plains All American: Undervalued, Reliable Midstream Operator
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Last year, I flagged Plains All American (NASDAQ:PAA) (PAGP) as a standout in the oil and gas midstream space. At the time, I said it was one of those stocks people tend to shrug off because of how GAAP accounting masks the company's real performance. And funnily enough, that shrugging continues. I still get comments about their revenue and net income volatility. I thought this overlooked ticker was a perfect for a contrarian trade.

And that bullish call aged pretty well. Since I published that piece last year, PAA is up 14.5% - that's more than double the S&P. Not bad for a so-called "boring" stock that makes its money moving oil and gas through long-term contracts. It's defensive, stable, and, apparently, profitable.

And here's the cherry on the top: 8.5% dividend. Put it all together, and those who gave PAA a closer look got a total return of 23%. That's more than triple what the S&P did over the same period. Honestly, it's one of the cleanest trades from last year. Sometimes, slow and steady really does win the race.

What Changed Since Our Last Piece

PAA has been busy since I wrote my last piece about twelve months ago. It's still the same, but some things have changed. First, the company increased its dividends by about 20%, from $1.25 to $1.52 annually.

But the highlight of the year was the company renegotiating a colossal of its long-haul crude oil contracts - specifically on pipelines like Cactus I, Cactus II, and Sunrise. The new deals came at lower rates, which understandably made some investors nervous. But thankfully, these lower rates were more than offset by rising volumes. The end result was flattish EBITDA. We'll talk about this more in the next section.

Keeping with tradition, PAA made some small, strategic acquisitions - what the industry likes to call "tuck-ins" that don't cost much. For example, they bought the remaining 43% of OMOG JV for $191 million, giving PAA full ownership. OMOG runs gathering pipelines in West Texas. Gathering pipelines is a great position to be in the midstream food chain. Suppliers might have options regarding which long-haul pipeline to use, depending on the destination, but all production must first go through the gathering pipelines owned by PAA.

Another tuck-in was the remaining 50% of Midway Pipeline LLC, which PAA bought for $90 million from Carl Icahn's struggling CVR (CVR). PAA immediately booked a $15 million capital gain on that deal. Not bad for a 16-inch pipeline that is just 100 miles, and offsetting risks tied to CVR.

In the fourth quarter of 2024, we recognized a gain of $15 million related to our acquisition of the remaining 50% interest in Midway Pipeline LLC - PAA's 2024 Annual Report

Finally, PAA boosted its stake in Saddlehorn Pipeline from 30% to 40%, paying $91 million. That values the 550-mile, 20-inch pipeline - stretching from Colorado to the Cushing hub - at $9.1 billion.

These acquisitions mostly came in late 2024, (except for Saddlehorn). So, their full impact hasn't fully shown up in the financials yet.

And here's the thing.

They did all that, while also decreasing leverage, buying back 18% of their Series A preferred stock.

Financials

PAA's crude oil segment delivered on expectations - reliable, steady, albeit incremental growth of 5%, reaching $2.28 billion, thanks to higher pipeline volumes - especially in the Permian (6%) - PAA's bread and butter - and also the Rocky Mountains (27%), a smaller, but fast-growing contributor. The federal government came to the rescue, offsetting the lower rates of long-haul - the private-sector-negotiated contracts, with the government-controlled tariffs set by the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission "FERC", which sets the rates on gathering pipelines. Gathering pipelines are considered utility assets, and are treated like, well, a utility. These rates increased in 2024, supporting the segment's adjusted EBITDA. Volumes running through PAA's pipelines rose by 5%.

The NGL segment adjusted EBITDA declined by 8%, contributing $480 million to total adjusted EBITDA or 14%. It's a small segment, but, large enough to impact consolidated growth figures. The main culprit? Oversupply. Prices dropped. PAA's NGL segment buys NGLs from producers, transports them through its pipelines, and profits from the price difference. This highlights, that while most of PAA's income comes from tariff-based contracts, it still has some exposure to oil and gas prices.

Canada Tariffs

With President Trump's recent tariffs on Canada, eyes have shifted to geopolitical risks spilling on PAA, raising concerns over the company's 2025 guidance. But here's the thing. Canada and the associated rocky mountain assets only make up about 15% of the total crude volumes running through PAA's pipelines. It's not nothing, but it's far from a dealbreaker.

Even if we do see a dip in Canadian oil flows, it's likely to be offset by rising production in the Permian and Delaware regions, the two domestic production hubs. And guess who controls a chunk of the gathering and long-haul pipelines in those regions? Yep, PAA.

Most importantly, I don't think Canada's cheap barrels are going to vanish overnight. Sure, there might be some rerouting or a slight slowdown, but the tariffs don't completely erase the economic appeal of Canada's cheap oil.

Valuation

As mentioned in the previous article, using GAAP net income or sales to value PAA is misleading. For a deeper dive, feel free to visit that piece. Adding to the complexity of valuing midstream oil and gas companies is the complex partnership structure. And this complexity extends beyond mark-to-market non-cash items, such as fill-in crude oil inside the pipelines, whose daily price fluctuations finds its way to the income statement.

Public investors who use headline earnings per share "EPS", price-to-earnings "P/E", price-to-sales "P/S", or even the company-provided adjusted EBITDA, should note that these ratios capture only part of the story. Behind the headline figures, there are private investors, preferred shareholders, and non-controlling interests - all of whom have real claims on the company's profits. The common shares and common units you see on the trading screens are only one of the multiple ownership stacks of midstream partnerships.

So, in this article, I did the heavy lifting and leveled most accounting discrepancies arising from different capital structures, making direct comparison between PAA and its peers more feasible. I stripped out preferred equity claims, which was simply done by deducting their claims over adjusted EBITDA. I calculated minority interest, mainly using figures from the balance sheet, but also using mandatory Net Income Attributed to Non-controlling Interest disclosures on SEC filings. Finally, I calculated EV/Adj. EBITDA using a cleaner, common equity-focused number.

Now, because different companies have different accounting treatments for the variables I used, some margin of safety is advised. However, directionally, my results are confirmed when using Price/Tangible Assets calculations using direct data from SEC filings, confirming that PAA's valuation falls below its peers.

Conclusion, and What Can Go Wrong?

PAA delivered spectacular results last year - stable income, rising volumes, a 20% dividend increase, and a 17% price appreciation. Here's the good part. Even after this 17% rally, and despite its flattish adjusted EBITDA growth, the stock is still undervalued. Why? This is one of those tickers that get shrugged quickly - volatile GAAP revenue and income, and they did cut their dividend back in 2015, and again in 2017, but that was when they were speculating on commodities. That was in the past, and we talked about this in last year's article. It's a new company now, with a new strategy, management, and business model.

The main risk is geopolitical. This article dismisses Canada's direct exposure - 15%. But Canada's oil running through the US crude system is much higher, and we didn't account for indirect exposure, assuming that US domestic production will, in the worst-case scenario, jump in to compensate for a slowdown in Canada's oil and gas exports. Since PAA operates in the major oil and gas production hubs in the US, slower capacity from Canada and higher capacity from domestic producers will balance out the utilization of PAA's pipelines.

Finally, in case of a recession, one would expect PAA's profitability to decline. While most of its earnings are contracted or FERC-regulated, it still maintains exposure to the transport tariff spot market and commodity trading.

Still, overall, I think this ticker has an attractive risk\reward profile.
